title: Why the US-China trade war lacks the guardrails of the past
gist: Cullen Hendrix argues the US-China trade war is structurally more dangerous than past trade wars (Pasta War, US-Japan) because the two sides are security rivals rather than allies, the fight is over intermediate inputs (rare earths, semiconductors) rather than finished goods, and both Trump and Xi treat the conflict as a test of personal legacy rather than an economic policy dispute.

themes.theme: Rhetoric vs. reality on US-China de-escalation

insights.claim: Despite Pete Hegseth's shift from warning of a Chinese Taiwan invasion (May 2025) to declaring US-China relations 'better than they've been in many years' after a Trump-Xi summit, the underlying structural risk of escalation has not actually diminished.
insights.speaker: Cullen S. Hendrix
insights.so_what: Investors should discount softer official rhetoric on US-China relations as a signal of reduced tail risk; the diplomatic tone has changed but the structural drivers of conflict have not.
insights.quote: US-China relations were "better than they've been in many years,"
insights.locator: US Secretary of Defense Pete Hegseth said in a May 2025 speech

insights.claim: The standard reassurances that trade wars rarely turn violent, that great powers have too much to lose, and that leaders act rationally, are either wrong or inapplicable to the current US-China standoff.
insights.speaker: Cullen S. Hendrix
insights.so_what: Standard mean-reversion assumptions used to price geopolitical risk (that things settle down because they always have) may not apply this cycle, warranting a higher persistent risk premium on US-China exposed assets.
insights.locator: The typical stylized facts that provide reassurance of eventual peaceful resolution

themes.theme: Why past trade wars stayed peaceful

insights.claim: Historical post-WWII trade wars, including the 1985-86 Pasta War (US vs. EEC) and the US-Japan trade disputes of the 1980s-90s, never escalated to armed conflict because the combatants were military allies of the United States, which put a hard ceiling on the conflict.
insights.speaker: Cullen S. Hendrix
insights.so_what: The historical base rate of 'trade wars rarely turn violent' is built on alliance-constrained cases and should not be extrapolated to non-allied strategic rivals like the US and China.
insights.locator: Many post-WWII trade wars have involved the United States and its security clients

insights.claim: US grand strategists tolerated import competition costs from Japan and Germany during the Cold War because having vibrant capitalist allies flanking the Soviet Union was judged more valuable than protecting domestic industry.
insights.speaker: Cullen S. Hendrix
insights.so_what: That strategic trade-off (tolerating economic pain for a security ally) has no analog with China, which is why the current conflict lacks the same self-limiting logic.
insights.locator: US grand strategists assessed that the costs of import competition

insights.claim: Trade wars are structurally easier to start than to end because the political economy of protectionism favors small, well-organized interest groups over diffuse consumers, biasing both domestic politics and interstate dynamics toward escalation rather than de-escalation.
insights.speaker: Cullen S. Hendrix
insights.so_what: Investors should expect tariff and export-control measures to ratchet upward over time rather than resolve quickly, since de-escalation requires overcoming embedded escalation incentives on both sides.
insights.locator: Trade wars are easier to start than end.

themes.theme: Intermediate goods as the new battlefield

insights.claim: Unlike pre-20th century trade wars that were fought over finished goods and market share, the modern global economy runs on trade in intermediate goods (like auto parts), which now exceeds trade in finished goods, meaning modern trade wars can cripple an economy's ability to produce anything at all rather than just shifting market share.
insights.speaker: Cullen S. Hendrix
insights.so_what: Supply-chain chokepoints in intermediate inputs are now the primary geopolitical weapon; investors should map portfolio exposure to single-source intermediate inputs (rare earths, specialty chemicals, advanced chips) rather than just finished-goods tariff exposure.
insights.locator: Pre-WWII trade wars were different in character.

insights.claim: China controls nearly 80 percent of the world's neodymium magnet production, a critical input spanning electric vehicles to guitar speakers, and its April 2025 export controls on rare earths and magnets targeted foreign manufacturers' ability to make finished goods at all, not just their competitiveness.
insights.speaker: Cullen S. Hendrix
insights.so_what: Rare-earth and magnet supply chains remain a high-conviction chokepoint trade: expect continued Western reshoring/diversification investment (e.g., MP Materials-type plays) and periodic EV/auto production disruption risk tied to Chinese export licensing decisions.
insights.quote: China produces nearly 80 percent of the world's neodymium magnets, a key input in everything from electric vehicles to guitar speakers.
insights.locator: Recent flashpoints over critical minerals and neodymium magnets

insights.claim: The rare-earth export control mechanism illustrates a cascading supply-chain logic: no neodymium leads to no magnets, no magnets leads to no motors, no motors leads to no electric vehicles, meaning the collateral damage of these controls reaches far beyond the intended target industry, hitting unrelated businesses like school music programs that can no longer afford student violins.
insights.speaker: Cullen S. Hendrix
insights.so_what: Second-order and third-order effects of critical mineral export controls will surface in unexpected sectors; investors should stress-test supply chains for indirect exposure to Chinese rare-earth choke points, not just direct EV/battery names.
insights.quote: No neodymium, no magnets; no magnets, no motors; no motors, no electric vehicles.
insights.locator: When China imposed export controls on rare earth materials

insights.claim: The author frames the shift from old-style trade wars to the current conflict as a shift from legible, symmetric 17th-century-style battles to medieval siege warfare, emphasizing the asymmetric, chokepoint-based nature of modern economic conflict.
insights.speaker: Cullen S. Hendrix
insights.so_what: Expect asymmetric, targeted chokepoint escalation (export bans on specific critical inputs) rather than broad symmetric tariff exchanges as the primary tool of future US-China economic conflict.
insights.quote: If old trade wars were 17th century–style battles—legible, fought by symmetrically armed and organized forces, the US-China trade war is more like medieval siege warfare.
insights.locator: A pre-WWII trade war could not have been waged this way

themes.theme: US-China as security rivals, not allies

insights.claim: The US-China trade war is historically unique because it involves security rivals rather than a patron and client, and the underlying competition is over strategic inputs determining future capability in AI and semiconductors, not just current finished-goods market share.
insights.speaker: Cullen S. Hendrix
insights.so_what: AI and semiconductor supply chains are the central strategic battleground of this conflict, implying sustained, bipartisan US policy support for chip export controls and domestic AI/semiconductor investment regardless of near-term diplomatic tone.
insights.locator: In contrast, the US-China trade war is historically unique

insights.claim: The pre-WWI Germany-UK trade war and arms race is the closest historical precedent to the current US-China dynamic, but even that analogy is imperfect since Germany and the UK continued substantial bilateral trade in finished and intermediate goods even while competing for spheres of influence in third markets, and their rivalry did not directly cause WWI's triggering assassination.
insights.speaker: Cullen S. Hendrix
insights.so_what: Historical analogies to WWI-era great power trade rivalry should be used cautiously as a risk framework; they suggest elevated tension but not a deterministic path to war.
insights.locator: The closest historical precedent might be the trade war and arms race

insights.claim: The US-China trading relationship remains the third-largest bilateral trade relationship in the world, and mutually-assured destruction plus continued economic interdependence still act as brakes on escalation to armed conflict.
insights.speaker: Cullen S. Hendrix
insights.so_what: Despite elevated structural risk, base-rate odds of near-term military conflict remain low given the sheer scale of continued bilateral trade dependency, a stabilizing factor investors shouldn't ignore.
insights.quote: the US-China trading relationship is still the third-largest bilateral trade relationship in the world
insights.locator: None of this means a US-China shooting war is very likely.

themes.theme: Leadership psychology as an escalation driver

insights.claim: Donald Trump and Xi Jinping are both unusually driven by personal legacy and historic destiny, viewing the trade war primarily as a test of will rather than an economic policy instrument, which means being seen to lose may feel more costly to each leader than the mounting economic damage itself.
insights.speaker: Cullen S. Hendrix
insights.so_what: Because both leaders' political incentives reward not backing down, investors should expect prolonged tariff/export-control conflict even when it is economically costly to both sides, and should be skeptical of quick resolution driven by pure economic rationality.
insights.quote: For both men, the trade war is not primarily an economic policy instrument. It is a test of will, and being seen to lose that test may feel more costly than any economic damage.
insights.locator: Very few people rise to positions like president

insights.claim: The absence of a shared security umbrella, credible diplomatic off-ramps or third-party mediators, and mutual recognition that the relationship is worth preserving means the historical mechanisms that resolved past trade wars peacefully are largely absent or severely weakened in the US-China case.
insights.speaker: Cullen S. Hendrix
insights.so_what: Do not expect a neutral third-party mediated resolution mechanism to defuse US-China tensions; any de-escalation will likely have to come from direct leader-to-leader bargaining, making outcomes more binary and headline-driven.
insights.locator: the US-China trade war is unusual in that the factors

insights.claim: The author explicitly states this analysis is not a general argument against economic openness or globalization, but is specific to the combination of deep economic integration with growing strategic rivalry between adversaries lacking guardrails.
insights.speaker: Cullen S. Hendrix
insights.so_what: This framework should be applied narrowly to US-China (and possibly other adversarial-but-integrated pairs) rather than used to justify broad-based economic nationalism or deglobalization theses across all trade relationships.
insights.quote: These are highly specific circumstances, and they provide no rationale for the vast majority of countries trading the vast majority of products to embrace economic nationalism.
insights.locator: These costs are not the costs of economic openness
